title: Karel Justinus Calewaert

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Belgian Roman Catholic bishop

| Karel Justinus Calewaert *Charles-Justin Calewaert* | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | (1893-10-17)17 October 1893  [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), East Flanders, Belgium |
| Died | 12 March 1963(1963-03-12) (aged 69)  [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), East Flanders, Belgium |
| Alma mater | [Catholic University of](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_Leuven_(1834%E2%80%931968) "Catholic University of Leuven (1834–1968)") [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven") / [Louvain](/wiki/Louvain,_Belgium "Louvain, Belgium") |
| Occupations | * [Priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest") * [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") |
| Predecessor | [Honoré Jozef Coppieters](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_Jozef_Coppieters "Honoré Jozef Coppieters") |
| Successor | [Léonce-Albert Van Peteghem](/wiki/L%C3%A9once-Albert_Van_Peteghem "Léonce-Albert Van Peteghem") |

**Karel Justinus Calewaert** (17 October 1893 – 27 December 1963) was a Belgian Catholic [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop").[[1]](#cite_note-PalEpi-1)[[2]](#cite_note-volgensNieuwsbladbe-2)

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karel_Justinus_Calewaert&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karel_Justinus_Calewaert&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

Calevaert was born in [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"),[[3]](#cite_note-graveinscription-3) a small town a short distance to the southwest of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"). His father, also named Justinus Calewaert, was a successful businessman,[[4]](#cite_note-KGK-4) with premises in the Tolpoortstraat, who also ran a distillery.[[2]](#cite_note-volgensNieuwsbladbe-2) When [war](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") broke out in 1914 Calevaert went initially to [England](/wiki/England "England"), but he later returned to Belgium and served as a stretcher-bearer on the front line.[[2]](#cite_note-volgensNieuwsbladbe-2)

He studied at St Hendrik's College in [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze") and at the [St Barbara College](/wiki/Sint-Barbaracollege "Sint-Barbaracollege"), a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") school in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), before moving on to the [Catholic University](/wiki/Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven "Katholieke Universiteit Leuven") of [Louvain](/wiki/Louvain,_Belgium "Louvain, Belgium").[[2]](#cite_note-volgensNieuwsbladbe-2) He was ordained into the priesthood on 23 April 1922.[[4]](#cite_note-KGK-4)

### Priest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karel_Justinus_Calewaert&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Priest")]

On 4 February 1925 he took a post as professor of [Moral theology](/wiki/Moral_theology "Moral theology") at the "CIBE"[[5]](#cite_note-ConvonWur-5) ([military academy](/wiki/Military_academy "Military academy")) in [Leopoldsburg](/wiki/Leopoldsburg "Leopoldsburg"), becoming director at this institution in 1927.[[6]](#cite_note-selonStHenri-6) On 1 October 1931 he was appointed president of the [Main Seminary](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") at [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), and in 1940 he became [Vicar general](/wiki/Vicar_general "Vicar general") for the diocese. In the context of the linguistic sensitivities of the time and place, the Ghent born composer [Ignace de Sutter](/w/index.php?title=Ignace_de_Sutter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ignace de Sutter (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ignace_de_Sutter "nl:Ignace de Sutter")] observed that Calewaert appeared to come from the Francophone class (*"....les gens d'expression française"*), although since he came from Deinze he also spoke perfectly good Flemish (Dutch).[[6]](#cite_note-selonStHenri-6)

### Bishop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karel_Justinus_Calewaert&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bishop")]

On 28 January 1948 Calewaert was appointed 27th [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of [Ghent](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Ghent "Roman Catholic Diocese of Ghent") in succession to [Honoré Jozef Coppieters](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_Jozef_Coppieters "Honoré Jozef Coppieters") who had died the previous month. Calewaert, now aged 54, was installed on 8 March 1948. He took as his motto "Caritate veritatis" (*"From Love of Truth"*).[[6]](#cite_note-selonStHenri-6)

On 17 April 1948 [Oscar Joliet](/wiki/Oscar_Joliet "Oscar Joliet") was appointed as Calewaert's [auxiliary bishop](/wiki/Auxiliary_bishop "Auxiliary bishop").

In 1950 he dedicated the St Paul Seminary at [Mariakerke](/wiki/Mariakerke_(East_Flanders) "Mariakerke (East Flanders)") in the chateau bequeathed by the aristocrat-politician [Amand Casier de ter Beken](/w/index.php?title=Amand_Casier_de_ter_Beken&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amand Casier de ter Beken (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amand_Casier_de_ter_Beken "nl:Amand Casier de ter Beken")], where seminarians could undertake the first two years of their training. On 16 May 1954 he dedicated the entire complex at [Mariagrot van Maldegem-Kleit](/w/index.php?title=Mariagrot_van_Maldegem-Kleit&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mariagrot van Maldegem-Kleit (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariagrot_van_Maldegem-Kleit "nl:Mariagrot van Maldegem-Kleit")].[[7]](#cite_note-deMariagrot-7)

During the 1955 schools conflict he wrote, on 8 July 1955, a pastoral letter calling on the faithful to support free education through the "School and family" programme, in respect of which a commemorative plaque was produced in 1958. Also in 1955, on 19 December 1955, it fell to Caelwaert to dedicate the important [Temse Bridge](/wiki/Temse_Bridge_(East_Flanders) "Temse Bridge (East Flanders)") at [Themse](/wiki/Themse "Themse") when it was opened by [The King](/wiki/Baudouin_of_Belgium "Baudouin of Belgium").

Karel Justinus Calewaert died on 12 March 1963, a couple of months after his 70th birthday. His body was buried in the crypt of his cathedral, [St Bavo's Cathedral, Ghent](/wiki/St_Bavo%27s_Cathedral,_Ghent "St Bavo's Cathedral, Ghent").
